observations.recommendation: a) We recommended that Management grant cash advance to officers and employees for their travelling expenses only except for those designated special disbursing officers who may be granted special cash advances for other legally authorized purposes and strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of P.D. 1445 and COA Circular 97-002 on the grant, utilization, and liquidations of cash advances and exhaust all possible means to settle cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management prioritize the implementation of programs/projects/activities funded out of the 20% Development Fund to ensure that the expected benefits for the intended recipients are timely achieved or delivered. By focusing on the efficient execution of these initiatives, the municipality can fulfill its obligations to the community and facilitate the timely realization of socio-economic benefits. observations.reason: Some development projects were not implemented due to geographical location of the municipality. Consequently, there were development projects not implemented within the targeted time schedule.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management implement and include more programs/projects/activities in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP).

Additionally, focus judiciously on planning how to accelerate the implementation of these initiatives to achieve the municipality's goals/targets concerning disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management. By expanding the scope of programs and projects within the LDRRMFIP and ensuring their timely execution, the municipality can enhance its disaster preparedness, response, and resilience efforts, ultimately safeguarding the lives and property of its constituents during calamities or disasters.
observations.managementAction:
Recommendations have been fully implemented.





Management implemented some of its programmed PPAs out of its LDRRM Fund. The Local Chief Executive ensured strategic planning on how to accelerate its implementation.
observations.reason: Some programs, projects and activities incurred delay of implementation due to the geographical location of the municipality and unpredictable weather conditions that hindered contractors from easy mobility of resources.

observations.recommendation:
We commended Management, particularly the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant, for their diligent efforts in ensuring the appropriate handling of the unexpended/unutilized balance of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) as of December 31, 2023, totaling ₱17,339,830.78. Their actions to transfer this balance to the Special Trust Fund (STF) and accurately record it in the books demonstrate a commitment to financial accountability and transparency.

Additionally, their proactive approach in monitoring the aging of annual unexpended balances is crucial for ensuring that funds are effectively utilized within the designated time frames. This ensures optimize resource allocation and strengthens the municipality's capacity to respond to disaster-related challenges effectively.

Overall, their exemplary performance in managing the LDRRMF underscores safeguarding public funds and enhancing disaster resilience within the community.
observations.managementAction: Management consistently adhered to existing rules and regulations to promote effective long-term financial planning and management of the LDRRMF.

In this connection, we assure you that no additional cash advances shall be allowed to any officials and employees unless previous cash advance given to him is settled and proper accounting treatment is made and ensure that advances for payroll and other cash advances shall be liquidated within the prescribed period from the grant of cash advances or upon serving the purposes thereof. Also, we commit our best effort for the collection of overdue accounts.

We appreciate your observation and recommendation to help our agency in taking necessary and appropriate actions to improve our internal control concerning Cash Advances.

Some accounts are related to timing of payments such as remittances of SEF share from RPT, unspent LDRRMF 2020 and etc. We have attached reconciliation statement as a result of our analysis and we commit to take appropriate adjustments to our report to attain validity and reliability of the recorded transactions.

Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer and GSO with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | We recognize that the reports submitted need to be reconciled in order to obtain accuracy and reliability of reports. The discrepancies in the stated amounts are caused by different basis in obtaining the reports from Municipal Engineering Office, General Services Office, as follows:

Records of Accounting reflects only the construction cost of roads funded from LGU Fund and Grants from NGAs, thus, reflected the actual disbursement.

Records of General Services Office on the Physical Count of Road Network reflects all cost of roads constructed regardless of road classification (local, provincial, national road) and sources of funds.

Records of Engineering Office reflects only estimated construction cost for the roads indicated therein.

With the above cited observations, we will commit to take necessary actions to correct or supplement the submitted reports, to wit:
Review subsidiary ledger from accounting records.
Review program of works and reports of GSO and Engineering Office.
Prepare reconciliation statement.
Prepare adjusting journal entries if necessary.

We hope that the above explanation and commitment may suffice your significant observation. We do appreciate your observation and it serves as a reminder to us in taking into consideration existing generally accepted accounting principles in attaining an accurate and fairly presented financial statements.

Furthermore, the Mucipal Accountant should always conduct pre audit and evaluate the submitted documents if appropriate and valid as proof  or evidence of claims before processing payment. 

Also, compute and impose liquidated damages to the contractor for the days of delay of the engineering consultancy service.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | We are submitting to your good office reports pertaining to site activities accomplished by the contractor particularly geotechnical, test pits result, and survey result to support the payment of engineering consultancy service and for your review and verification. The Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed were already submitted upon payment of the 15% service fee as documents necessary to support the disbursement. Moreover, the contractor requested 45 days of extension of Contract from November 6 to December 21, 2020 due to unforeseen events, as follows:

Fourteen-day mandatory quarantine of their manpower due to COVID19 pandemic which hindered in the timely start and hauling of equipment to project site.

Fortuitous event – Low Pressure Area and Northeast monsoon in Divilacan during the delivery of drilling equipment from Sta. Ana, Cagayan to project site.

We also prepared reconciliation of the billing statement to the terms of agreement stipulated in the contract.

We regard your utmost understanding in the delays of implementation of our programs and projects. We commit to adhere to the existing rules and regulations to avoid violations and irregularities in relation to fiscal matters.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management thru the BAC expedite the conduct of public biddings of all the infrastructure projects of the Municipality that are included in the approved AIP  and ensure that the procurement process be strictly followed such as:(1) advertise and/or post the invitation to bid; (2) conduct pre-procurement and pre-bid conferences; (3) determine the eligibility of prospective bidders (4) receive bids; (5) conduct the evaluation of bids; (6) undertake post qualification proceedings; (7) recommend awards of contract to the Head of procuring entity, etc.
observations.managementAction:
AAR2020 | At the outset, please be informed that at the beginning of 1st Quarter of the year just upon approval of the APP, this Committee had conducted series of pre-procurement conferences pertaining to all approved Purchase Request to thresh out readiness of documents and to verify the conformity of scheduled period of implementation and subsequently all PRs were posted at PhilGEPS Portal for Invitation to prospective Bidders. Despite all exhausted efforts such as Re-Posting, ABC’s revisions, Resolution to undergo Other Mode of Procurements (Sec. 53 of RA 9184) and employed all avenues to inform and encourage possible bidders, yet, still few from outside locality were interested and participated in the implementation of projects (Please see attached printed copies of IAEBs). There are no qualified bidders within the locality only Pakyaw Groups are available and very much willing to undergo the construction activities but lacks the necessary juridical and legal documents required under RA 9184. Interested qualified bidders from outside locality were also hesitant to come in to Divilacan due to travel/activity restrictions brought about by Covid-19 Global Pandemic protocols and the geographical isolation of the locality. 

In the abovementioned premise which mainly resulted in a low implementation of projects versus the targeted approved APP, this Committee is simply reasoning and presenting that such result is unmistakably beyond its control. However, with the unending effort of this Committee to invite prospective Bidders outside the locality, an additional prospective Contractor had signified its willingness to enter into the locality to participate in our scheduled biddings. In addition, the recent installation of an Internet Connection in the LGU had lessen the burden of the BAC in posting of IAEB. Previously, the BAC Secretariat shall have to travel to either Ilagan or Cauayan purposely to post at PhilGEPS Portal.  Finally, the HOPE had instructed this Committee to facilitate all needed documents in order to fast tract implementation of projects by respective End-Users/Implementers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Project In-Charge on Committee of the programs, projects and activities of COVID-19 Bayanihan Grant, the Project Engineer and BAC strictly monitor the proper implementation of the contract entered into, and for PPAs target schedule deadlines, and always comply with the set deadlines to avoid delays. Also, impose observance and compliance with Item 5.1 of 5.0 - Project Implementation of Local Budget Circular No.128 dated September 17, 2020 also known as Republic Act No.11494.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2020 | This has reference to your Audit Observation Memorandum No. 2021-002 dated February 15, 2021 which is in line with the Municipality’s Bayanihan to Recover as One Act inefficiency to implement program and activities for the COVID 19 relief and recovery activities. 

Upon coordination with the contractor, hence cited the following reasons that brought out to delay of the said project, to wit:

Community quarantine measures that imposed stringent limitations on movement and transportation of people to enforce community quarantine protocols thus also hindered the transport of construction materials; and

Transportation of essential commodities was being prioritized by the small cargo vessel from Sta. Ana Cagayan to Divilacan.

However, strict monitoring of the project “Recovery and Treatment Facility Building” will be observed, to fast track the completion of the said project. Rest assured that we will do our very best to work as one for a common goal and purpose.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that the 20% DF shall be optimally utilized for the projects, programs and activities (PPAs) to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management which shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020 | We will assess and improve our planning and strategies in implementing programs and projects.  Hence, we will strictly monitor and fast track the implementation and completion of all projects and coordinate with the contractors to closely monitor its accomplishment for the prompt implementation and timely completion of all awarded and contracted projects.

We will also coordinate with the contractors to closely monitor its accomplishment for the prompt implementation and timely completion of all awarded and contracted projects. We commit to have better plans and timelines for every project to guarantee prompt completion of infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management through the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (MDRRMO) and Municipal Engineer strictly monitor the implementation of programmed projects and ensure completion within the set timelines/deadlines in order that Disaster Preparedness and Disaster Response effectively served and help save lives, reduce future risk, human and material losses.
observations.managementAction:

Hence, we would like to furnish you an updated Report of Accomplishment on Infrastructure Projects for CY 2020 (By Contract and Administration) with specified fund sources, project status as well as its physical accomplishment.

We would strictly monitor the implementation of the said projects for its completion the soonest possible time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management maximize the utilization and implementation of the LDRRMF approved PPAs in strengthening and support disaster preparedness and response to be able to achieve the forecasted targets and deliver its goal and purpose.
observations.managementAction:
AAR

 2020 | Apparently, the incidence
 of Coronavirus-19 pandemic entailed adverse impact to the Philippine economy and society causing the declaration of state of public health emergency throughout the Philippines and has set off the guidelines on Risk Based Public Health Standards Mitigation.  

In compliance with the directives from higher authorities, the local government unit was consumed and overwhelmed in its role in the localized implementation for covid-19 response and recovery interventions and providing mechanisms to accelerate the recovery and bolster the resiliency of the town of Divilacan including fund allocation.  The rise of confirmed cases outside municipality and the serious threat to the health, safety, security, and lives of our constituents persisted until the first semester of 2020.   Thus, it implied the discontinuance of some appropriated programs or PPAs which cannot be utilized effectively as a result of the covid-19 outbreak.

However, in order to maximize the utilization and strengthening of disaster preparedness and response, the LDRRMC as a major key player in working for the prevention management and combat of said pandemic, major adjustment and reconsideration of priorities were undertaken to address the Covid-19 pandemic.  

A reprogramming was presented through an LDRRMC Resolution No.02-2020 adopted through an Appropriation Ordinance No. 012-2020 appropriated under the 70% mitigation fund, however still some of the projects or 49.89% left unutilized as per audit observation mentioned earlier.  


The said pandemic greatly impeded the implementation since most of the remaining projects embodied in this entailed associating such as construction/improvement of evacuation centers (continuing PPAs) and conduct of trainings and community-based management    system which was against the protocols.  Sea voyage was banned where various construction materials are being transported, even after it was lifted only essential goods were actually given priority.   

At our ends, we could only hope and wait the pandemic to end while all other PPAs remain unobligated but it persisted until the end of the first semester of 2020.

observations.recommendation: We advised that the Treasury Department enforce the collection of real property taxes to delinquent tax payers. Apply the collection strategy of collection of the Real Property Tax. The tax starts to accrue on the first of January every year and can be paid in four installments before the office of the local treasurer on or before the end of each quarter. A discount (10% or sometimes 20%) is available to taxpayers who pay the tax in advance while late payments are subject to a two percent (2%) surcharge per month, to a maximum of 36%.If necessary issue demand letters and apply the remedies as provided in Section 256 of P.D. No.7160.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2019 Part III | Management acknowledged the audit observation and commented that the low collection efficiency was due to the following reasons:

Some of the properties declared were forest zone and cultivation of said areas are restricted, they become idle lands. Therefore, the property owners are hesitant to pay their obligations.
Duplication of real property tax declaration. Properties that were sold by original owners were not cancelled instead it was declared as new property.
Unknown addresses of real property owners. Demand letters were sent through post office but it returned back to sender due to lacking of information of address.
The Municipality is not yet tax map. 
Also, strategies have been applied in order to increase the collection efficiency of RPT such as:

Conducts information and education campaign on the 12 barangays of this municipality including secondary school students.
Giving of shirts as incentive to early tax payers.
Posting of tarpaulins to conspicuous places as a reminder on the manner of payments.
Posting of delinquent real property owners to different areas like Provincial, Municipal and Barangay halls.
Issuance of real property tax bills and issuance of demand letters to tax payers. 
 In addition, we intensified information education campaign on the importance of paying RPT and we issued demand letters to delinquent tax payers. 

Rejoinder:

Though management applied several collection strategies as mentioned above still our observations and analysis disclosed minimal impact in the collection of RPT receivables. We compared the RPT Receivables to establish collection efficiencies as follows:

observations.recommendation: 1.6    We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prioritize the preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statements, prepare a Request Letter address to the Banks Manager of its authorized depository bank requesting the timely issuances of Bank Statements with all supporting documents after the end of each month for the timely preparation of the BRSs to be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for a timely review and audit. Also, observe strict compliance with the rules and regulations on the preparation and submission of the BRSs within the prescribed period.

2.4     We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer monitor the status of all checks issued and encashed so that those checks that are already considered “STALE” shall be marked cancelled; furnish the Municipal Accountant the list of stale checks for the reversion of the amounts to Accounts Payable.  After the cancellation of the stale checks, the Management shall inform the payees to surrender the stale check in their possession for replacement of a new negotiable check. Both the new replacement checks and stale checks shall be reported in the RCI.

3.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Facilitate the tracing and locating of relevant documents to serve as basis in adjusting identified reconciling items in the respective reciprocal accounts;
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and
Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to deter error and omissions.

4.7	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Assessor facilitate the updating of the RPT Account Register and Taxpayers Index Card to come-up with a comprehensive Certified List of the Taxpayers (CLT) which will be submitted to the Municipal Accountant as basis in the setting-up of the RPT and SET Receivables as required under Section 20 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I;

5.7	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in determining the actual status of all reported completed and on-going infrastructure projects and to effect the necessary adjustment in the books of accounts. Based on the result of the evaluation and validation,  reflect the accurate balance of the CIP accounts and ensure that all completed and paid projects are immediately classified to their appropriate PPE accounts, also, compute and record the appropriate/correct depreciation expenses.

6.8 	We recommended that the Management cause the immediate submission of contract documents and all paid DVs with complete supporting documents particularly infrastructure projects which are already delay/overdue to the Office of the Auditor for us to expedite the conduct of audit, contract review and requesting of inspection of completed infrastructure projects. Moreover, strictly comply with the prescribed period of submission provided under Section 6.05 of COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995.

7.6	We recommended that Management ensure that payments of mobilization expenses to contractors be supported with complete documentation as prescribed to prove the validity, legality and propriety thereof.
7.7	As regards the mobilization expenses, the corresponding letter- requests and the irrevocable letters of credit or bank guarantees from/issued by the latter be submitted for verification. Should the documents be not submitted or found not in order, the disbursements may be suspended or disallowed in audit.

8.6      We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Assign an official or employee to conduct pre-inspection of the properties subject for repair and post-inspection of the work accomplished and submit the corresponding evaluation or inspection reports thereof, and
ensure that all procurements for repairs and maintenance are supported with complete documentation to prove the validity, legality and propriety of the transactions. It is emphasized that completeness of documentation includes not only the submission of the required documents but also the completeness of the details thereof.

9.8	We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Accountant cause the immediate submission of all financial reports to the Office of the Auditor and strictly comply with the prescribed period of submission provided under Section 70 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I, Items 2.1 and 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 and Section 122 of P.D.No.1445, to ensure timely conduct of review, analysis and validation of Management’s assertions on the fairness of presentation of Financial Statements and to communicate the results thereof to concerned officials.

10.5	We recommended that Management direct:
a) 	The Budget Officer to ensure that all departments/offices submit their respective PPMPs and that these are properly reviewed.  The deficiencies noted in the evaluation of the same should be properly communicated to the concerned department/office for rectification and compliance, such that budget proposal and consolidated APP by the BAC is accurate and reliable; and
b) 	The GSO to procure the supplies and materials requirements in accordance with the approved APP/PPMP.  Should there be additional requisitions, require the revision of the PPMP of the concerned department/office in coordination with the budget office and the BAC for proper control and monitoring and to ensure that only the requirements included in the APP/PPMP are procured.

11.6	We recommended that the BAC follow the provisions of Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic (IRR) Act No. 9184 in all its procurement activities to avail the most advantageous price for the municipal government. In addition, the Municipal Accountant is enjoined to examine completeness of supporting documents for every transaction of the municipality before payment to ensure validity, propriety and legality of the disbursements.

12.6    We recommended that Management require the GSO to provide the complete details/specifications of the items in the Purchase Request, as such, necessary information are included in the instruction to prospective bidders/suppliers to ensure that the items in the PO are the items requested and delivered. Moreover, ensure that all required information in the POs were properly and completely accomplished.

13.6 	We recommended that the Management fast track the prompt implementation and completion of all programmed development projects. The concerned LGU officials should monitor closely the accomplishments of its contractors to ensure the efficient and prompt implementation of all contracted development projects and programs under the 20% DF that are envisioned to help in the attainment of social, economic and environmental development of the Municipality.
13.7 Also, planning and proper timing in the implementation of infrastructure projects should be observe at all times by the LGU specifically during summer season where the project areas are 100 percent workable to guarantee prompt completion of the infrastructure projects.

15.7  	We recommended that the Management cause or direct the Municipal Planning and Development Office, in coordination  with  the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to facilitate the updating of its existing GAD database to have an effective basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data for proper identification of existing gender issues to ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GPB are responsive to address the same.

17.6 	We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan through the Committee on Women and Family, and with the assistance of GAD Focal Point System Executive Committee and Technical Working Group, prioritize the formulation and passing of GAD Code based on the gender issues and concerns in their respective locality and promote, protect and fulfill women’s empowerment and gender equality pursuant to PCW Memorandum Circular No. 2009-1, Republic Act No. 9710 and the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

19.6 	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide regularly copies of RSUDRRMF to the MDRRMO, for them to be well informed of the accurate balance of DRRM Funds available for appropriation as their basis in the preparation of LDRRMFIP. Also, observe and maintain proper coordination for an  effective implementation of the PPAs pertaining to risk reduction preparedness and mitigation.

20.5  	We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Accountant prepare and certify the RSUDRRMF to ensure strict compliance on the submission of monthly RSU of the LDRRMF to the Office of the Auditor as mandated under item 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

21.3 We recommended that the Municipal SWMB prepare, formulate and submit the same the yearly specific plans and programs to support the activities in the attainment of its targets as laid down in the Municipality’s 10-year SWMP.

They admitted that they did not render request from the COA for write-off for the dormant or non-moving account due to some lacking requirement such as death certificate, affidavit of insolvency etc. They have sent demand letters to the accountable officers however, the said persons could not be located despite diligent efforts to find them. Also, the two accountable officers namely, Mr.Rufino Gonzales and Mr. Jerry Ojenar were already passed away.

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant commit to take the necessary actions such as verification, analysis and validation of the doubtfulness of the receivable, prepare aging for dormant receivables, determine items to be included in the request for write-off, and prepare the necessary adjusting entries upon approval of the request for write-off by COA.

observations.recommendation: We advised that the Treasury Department enforce the collection of real property taxes to delinquent tax payers. Apply the collection strategy of collection of the Real Property Tax. The tax starts to accrue on the first of January every year and can be paid in four installments before the office of the local treasurer on or before the end of each quarter. A discount (10% or sometimes 20%) is available to taxpayers who pay the tax in advance while late payments are subject to a two percent (2%) surcharge per month, to a maximum of 36%.If necessary issue demand letters and apply the remedies as provided in Section 256 of P.D. No.7160.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018 Observation No.02 | Management acknowledged the audit observation and commented that the low collection efficiency was due to the following reasons:

Some of the properties declared were forest zone and cultivation of said areas are restricted, they become idle lands. Therefore, the property owners are hesitant to pay their obligations.
Duplication of real property tax declaration. Properties that were sold by original owners were not cancelled instead it was declared as new property.
Unknown addresses of real property owners. Demand letters were sent through post office but it returned back to sender due to lacking of information of address.
The Municipality is not yet tax map. 
Also, strategies have been applied in order to increase the collection efficiency of RPT such as:

Conducts information and education campaign on the 12 barangays of this municipality including secondary school students.
Giving of shirts as incentive to early tax payers.
Posting of tarpaulins to conspicuous places as a reminder on the manner of payments.
Posting of delinquent real property owners to different areas like Provincial, Municipal and Barangay halls.
Issuance of real property tax bills and issuance of demand letters to tax payers. 
 In addition we intensified information education campaign on the importance of paying RPT and we issued demand letters to delinquent tax payers. 

Rejoinder:

Though management applied several collection strategies as mentioned above still our observations and analysis disclosed minimal impact in the collection of RPT receivables. We compared the RPT Receivables to establish collection efficiencies as follows:
    
RPT–Dec.31,2018    P 2.36 million
RPT–Dec.31,2018    P 2.35 million
Difference                    0.01 million

Also, most of the time they encounter failure of bidding since, no contractors were interested to participate in the bidding process because of far location, unavailability of road and incurring additional cost.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management facilitate the titling of the land and strictly comply with the provisions of laws on the proper transfer of titles of acquired properties in favor of LGU-Divilacan in order to protect its interest. Create a committee to handle the activities and seek legal assistance from the Provincial Legal Officer, if necessary. 

Furthermore, seek approval of the Survey Plans and technical descriptions of the acquired ROW by the Director of Lands and have them filed with the Register of Deeds for annotation thereof in the landowners’ certificates of title pursuant to PD No.1529 and Section 15 of EO 1035. Deeds of Absolute Sale and donations should likewise be properly notarized and registered with the Register of Deeds to protect the interest of the government.
observations.managementAction:
Management admitted that they failed to observe proper procedures to facilitate the proper titling of some LGU acquired lots and failure of the GSO to maintain property cards indicating the lot description, number, and areas. However, they assured us of the existence and regularity of transactions pertaining to lots.

Also, Management informed us that they will form committees to conduct inventories for the abovementioned properties and to secure necessary documents for the transfer of corresponding certificate of titles.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to monitor and evaluate the utilization of the LDRRMF to ensure implementation of all the pre-disaster preparedness programs, projects and activities.
observations.managementAction: Management acknowledged the inefficiency and ineffectiveness to fully implement the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMF) particularly on the 70% or Mitigation Fund. However, they informed us that they will review plans under the Mitigation Fund to give priorities in the implementation of various programs and activities that could enhance the capability of the LGU to alleviate the effect of unforeseen calamities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management comply with the submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, as provided in Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, in order to monitor compliance on accounting and reporting of the utilization of LDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The Management accepted the non-submission of LDRRMF utilization reports within the prescribe period using the prescribed forms. Also, they choose to submit the LDRRMF reports on a quarterly basis to minimize expenses in the submission on reports. On the other hand, they assured us compliance with the submission of said reports on time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management through the Municipal Treasurer strictly enforce the collection of real property taxes to delinquent tax payers. Apply the collection strategy of collection of the Real Property Tax. The tax starts to accrue on the first of January every year and can be paid in four installments before the office of the local treasurer on or before the end of each quarter. A discount (10% or sometimes 20%) is available to taxpayers who pay the tax in advance while late payments are subject to a two percent (2%) surcharge per month, to a maximum of 36%.
Issue demand letters, if necessary, and apply the remedies provided in Section 256 of P.D. No.7160.
We recommended that the Management submit the deficient documents for technical review and evaluation process for generating timely and relevant audit results.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant issue Certificate of Availability of Funds (CAF) for all contracts of infrastructure projects as mandated under Section 168, GAAM Volume I, COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management comply with the following:
Submit the Monthly Report Of Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption every end of the month
Ensure that all supporting documents especially the Driver’s Trip Tickets (DTT) are properly and completely accomplished and transmitted for audit; and
Establish and implement effective control measures in the use of government vehicle and fuel consumption as provided in COA Circular No. 77-61

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that Management:
Evaluate the viability of identified priority PPAs during the planning phase and monitor the implementation of such PPAs;
Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund allocated for the implementation of various projects, programs and activities identified as priorities pursuant to DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 to help achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes and shall partake the nature of investment or capital expenditure; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to revert to unappropriated surplus of the GF, the unobligated balance of continuing appropriation from CYs 2008-2016 as of December 31, 2018 in accordance with the COA Circular No. 89-302 dated December 29, 1989.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point Officer submit the GPB and AR reports on/or before the deadline to the Office of the Auditor for us to have ample time to verify/confirm the veracity and reliability of said reports and exercise our audit functions.

We recommended to the Management that:
the basic services/priority expenditures enumerated above should be considered in the implementation of fund so as not to deprive the constituents of its benefit. Maximize utilization of the Municipality’s Special Education Fund (SEF).
the Municipal Budget Officer in coordination with the Local School Board prepare its SEF budget for proper allocation of its fund and taking into consideration the allowable expenses chargeable against SEF or PPAs set forth on the provisions of RA 7160 and DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.

We recommend that the Bids and Awards Committee and the General Services Office to submit copies of approved contracts, purchase orders, together with supporting documents within five (5) working days after their perfection regardless of the amount and Notice of Deliveries within twenty four (24) hours from acceptance to the Audit Team to ensure timely review/evaluation of the contracts and inspection of deliveries, respectively.

We recommended that the Management facilitate the creation and organization of its IAS to help Management in directing and strengthening of the Internal Control System of LGU-Divilacan in their operations and to achieve effective and efficient administration and functions.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned officers and employees to liquidate/settle immediately their outstanding cash advances by:

Demanding the immediate submission of duly accomplished liquidation reports.
Preparing demand letters to be issued to concerned officers and employees for cash advances that not yet settled/liquidated within the timeframe for the submission of liquidation reports, by the Municipal Accountant and noted by the Local Chief Executive, copy furnished the Audit Team. In case of non-compliance, direct the Municipal Accountant to withhold the payment of their salaries, or any money due to them.
Sending the final demand letters to persons who have retired or out of office who still have unliquidated cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The Local Government of Divilacan took note of the audit observations and assured us that they will require/demand liquidations of outstanding cash advances of concerned officers and employees. In cases of retired and out of office they agreed to issue demand/final demand letters.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the balances of Due from/Due to Local Government Units appearing in the books of Provincial Government of Isabela (PGI) against LGU-Divilacan and make the necessary/appropriate adjustment.
observations.managementAction: The Management committed to implement our recommendation.

Management acknowledged the non-reconciliation of GSO and Accounting records despite of series of physical count conducted by the GSO but they assure us of the accuracy of the general ledgers being maintained by the Accounting Office since all payments pertaining to those transactions were accounted for in the book of accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LGU-Divilacan include in the budget proposal the allocation of 5% of its annual budget to support its  GAD Plan, which shall contain in details the programs and activities for the whole year as provided in the Joint Circular No.2004-1 of the Department of Budget and Management (DBM), National Development Authority (NEDA) and National Commission on the Role of Filipino Women (NCRFW)     

Furthermore, obliged the GAD Focal Point Person and other concerned Official to implement the GAD projects and mechanisms to achieve the objectives of the government regarding the institutionalization of Gender and Development in the communities. Also, the GAD Council should strictly conform to the guidelines for the preparation of Annual Gender and Development (GAD) Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report. The same should be geared towards the development of programs, activities and projects that promote gender-responsive governance, protect and fulfill women’s human rights, and promote women’s economic empowerment.
observations.managementAction:
Management explained while it was true that there was no separate appropriation for GAD for Annual Budget CY 2017, the management addressed gender issues in their municipality through the implementation of programs and activities incorporated in the MOOE and Capital Outlay of different Offices. Further, their Annual Budget for CY 2018 contains separate appropriation for GAD and GAD plan and budget approved and endorsed by DILG.   

Furthermore, the GAD Focal Point System will implement programs and activities in conformity with the Joint Circular No.2004-1 of DBM, NEDA and NCRFW. Also, the GAD Focal Person will consolidate the accomplishment reports of different offices implementing GAD programs and activities for monitoring and evaluation of results.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 particularly on the implementation of its infrastructure projects through public bidding in order to obtain better bargains for the government and to protect its interest.

We recommended that the Municipality establish LCPC and allocate at least 1% of its IRA for that purpose in compliance with Section 15 of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 to ensure implementation of programs, projects and activities for the protection of children.
observations.managementAction:
While it is true that their Municipality failed to allocate 1% of the internal revenue allotment for the mentioned purpose, the Management delivered basic services for the promotion of rights and welfare of children, establishment of playgrounds for children each day care centers, conduct of children’s month celebration with parents to strengthen parental care and guidance among children and other programs and activities related thereto.

Moreover, the Management informed us that they have already appropriated 1% of the internal revenue allotment for the budget year 2018. Also, Local Council for the Protection of Children was already organized and functional since 2017 and ensures that delivery of basic services was met thru funding from Gender and Development Fund and other programs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all department heads involved in development projects to assure the implementation of prioritized infrastructure projects under the 20 percent development fund. Utilize the unexpended balances of P56 million to boost the improvement of socio-economic development of Municipality of Divilacan for the benefits of its constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management agreed with the audit observation, they informed us that they are  on the process of identifying/ verifying the available cash equivalent for them to reprogrammed the identified projects this CY 2018 to address the delay of implementation of prioritize infrastructure projects that were funded under prior year’s 20 percent Development Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Budget Officer to establish separate Registry of Appropriation, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for the Quick Response Fund (QRF), Maintenance and Other Operating Expense (MOOE) and Capital Outlay (CO) of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF).
observations.managementAction: Management admitted the failure of the Budget Office personnel to follow proper reporting for appropriation, allotments and obligations. However, they commit thru the Budget and Accounting Office to established separate Registries of Appropriations.

observations.recommendation: We advised that the Local Chief Executive ensure that the 20% EDF and Continuing Appropriations are optimally utilized for its intended purposes for the development of the municipality and its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The LGU Management thru the Municipal Development Council reviewed the balances of unimplemented and implemented programs and projects and prepared plans and programs to utilize the 20% EDF supported by appropriation ordinances.

observations.recommendation:
1.7	We recommended that Management direct the concerned officers and employees to liquidate/settle immediately their outstanding cash advances by:
Demanding the immediate submission of duly accomplished liquidation reports.
Preparing demand letters to be issued to concerned officers and employees for cash advances that not yet settled/liquidated within the timeframe for the submission of liquidation reports, by the Municipal Accountant and noted by the Local Chief Executive, copy furnished the Audit Team. In case of non-compliance, direct the Municipal Accountant to withhold the payment of their salaries, or any money due to them.
Sending the final demand letters to persons who have retired or out of service who still have unliquidated cash advances.
1.8	Management continue to remind all accountable officers and employees to strictly abide with the provisions of Section 89 of PD No. 1445 on the timely liquidation of cash advances of the concerned officials/employees; and refrain from granting cash advances to employees not connected with the LGU. The “No Liquidation No Cash Advance Policy” should be strictly enforced by the Management.

2.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the balances of Due from/Due to Local Government Units appearing in the books of Provincial Government of Isabela (PGI) against LGU-Divilacan and make the necessary/appropriate adjustment.

3.6	We recommended that Management direct the Department Heads concerned to undertake the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant  record the required adjustments taking up the transfer of said completed infrastructure projects costing P17,304,787.00 including the related depreciation and accumulated depreciation, from the LGU-Divilacan to the concerned  Barangay books of accounts. Consequently, require the Barangay Bookkeeper  to take up the corollary adjustments in the Barangay books of accounts and maintain the required subsidiary records.
The Municipal General Services Officer  and Municipal Accountant fast track the identification and listing of buildings, multi-purpose halls, community centers, and other structures from the years 2017 down owned by LGU-Divilacan but used by barangays, for subsequent adjustment.
The Municipal General Services Officer  furnish detailed schedules of these structures to the Barangays concerned for reconciliation with the inventory reports (RPCPPE) and the actual accountability of each barangay.
Prepare Certificate of Acceptance and MOA between the LGU-Divilacan & concerned Barangays for the proper maintenance or possible repair of the structures and other terms & conditions specify in the MOA.

5.5	We recommended that the LGU-Divilacan include in the budget proposal the allocation of 5% of its annual budget to support its  GAD Plan, which shall contain in details the programs and activities for the whole year as provided in the Joint Circular No.2004-1 of the Department of Budget and Management (DBM), National Development Authority (NEDA) and National Commission on the Role of Filipino Women (NCRFW)
5.6	Furthermore, obliged the GAD Focal Point Person and other concerned Official to implement the GAD projects and mechanisms to achieve the objectives of the government regarding the institutionalization of Gender and Development in the communities. The same should be geared towards the development of programs, activities and projects that promote gender-responsive governance, protect and fulfill women’s human rights, and promote women’s economic empowerment.

6.7	We recommended that the Management strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 particularly on the implementation of its infrastructure projects through public bidding in order to obtain better bargains for the government and to protect its interest.

7.4	We recommended that the Management (i) maximize the utilization of SEF Fund, implement and monitor the implementation of projects in order not to deprive the intended beneficiaries of the said projects, (ii)  make proper representation with the Dep-Ed principals to facilitate the downloading of funds for the renovation, repairs and maintenance of school buildings and facilities to attain an environment conducive to learning and prevent the disruption of classes during rainy season.

8.5	We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to submit at the beginning of the year the list of all on-going government projects/programs/activities (“PPA”) and those that are to be implemented during the year, and also to enable the Audit Team to comply with the submission of the Quarterly Report of the PPAs to the Regional Director for monitoring compliance, pursuant with the above cited regulations.

9.7	We recommended that the Municipality establish LCPC and allocate at least 1% of its IRA for that purpose in compliance with Section 15 of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012  to ensure implementation of programs, projects and activities for the protection of children.

10.5	We recommended that the Management fast track the implementation  of its  Ecological Solid Waste Management Program by utilizing the appropriated funds to fully attain the objectives under Republic Act 9003.

11.8	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all department heads involved in development projects to monitor and fast track the implementation and completion of programmed projects and to ensure that funds allocated thereon are utilized for projects identified in the approved AIP, in accordance with the guidelines/policies prescribed under DILG/DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011, as superseded by DILG-DBM JMC 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.
11.9	The Engineering Department and other department heads concern should also be instructed to include in the AIP, those that are proposed for implementation for a particular year, determined as priority projects and adequately funded in the approved budget. Any additional funds generated during the year may be allocated to other programs and projects as approved by the Sanggunian.
11.10	Likewise, monitor the implementation of the identified priority programs, projects and activities that are to be funded out of the 20 percent Development Fund in line with the proposals of the local development committee that should regularly convene to update or modify the priority programs as called for under existing circumstances and changing conditions.

12.4	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct all department heads involved in development projects to assure the implementation of prioritized infrastructure projects under the 20 percent development fund. Utilize the unexpended balances of P56 million to boost the improvement of socio-economic development of Municipality of Divilacan for the benefits of its constituents.

13.5	We recommended that the Management facilitate the titling of the land and strictly comply with the provisions of laws on the proper transfer of titles of acquired properties in favor of LGU-Divilacan in order to protect its interest. Create a committee to handle the activities and seek legal assistance from the Provincial Legal Officer, if necessary.
13.6	Furthermore, seek approval of the Survey Plans and technical descriptions of the acquired ROW by the Director of Lands and have them filed with the Register of Deeds for annotation thereof in the landowners’ certificates of title pursuant to PD No.1529 and Section 15 of EO 1035. Deeds of Absolute Sale and donations should likewise be properly notarized and registered with the Register of Deeds to protect the interest of the government.

14.6	We recommended that Management require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) to monitor and evaluate the utilization of the LDRRMF to ensure implementation of all the pre-disaster preparedness programs, projects and activities.

15.4	We recommended that the Management comply with the submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, as provided in Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, in order to monitor compliance on accounting and reporting of the utilization of LDRRMF.

16.5	We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Budget Officer to establish separate Registry of Appropriation, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for the Quick Response Fund (QRF), Maintenance and Other Operating Expense (MOOE) and Capital Outlay (CO) of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF).
